gompers2020vc.title: How VCs Make Decisions
gompers2020vc.summaryPlain: Research Design and Survey Innovation Gompers, Gornall, Kaplan, and Strebulaev conducted comprehensive survey of 885 venture capital investors across US and Europe, directly documenting how VCs make investment decisions and allocate time. This is among the largest VC surveys ever conducted, covering major venture capital firms representing substantial fraction of global VC activity. The survey asks VCs to rank factors in deal evaluation (team, market, technology, etc.), quantify time spent on various activities, document board composition decisions, and detail deal sourcing patterns. Prior research relied on retrospective analysis of completed investments or case studies, introducing selection bias and requiring inference about decision-making processes from outcomes. Direct survey of VC investors provides unique perspective on actual decision-making criteria and activities. The paper's central finding—team quality is the #1 investment factor—represents surprising magnitude: 94% of VCs rank team most important, well ahead of market (87%) and technology (87%). This team emphasis raises questions about what VCs mean by "team" and how thoroughly they evaluate founder/management capability. Team Factor: Definition and Evaluation Depth The overwhelming emphasis on "team" raises natural question: what do VCs mean by team, and how thoroughly do they evaluate team quality? The paper documents that VCs focus on founder background, experience, track record, and industry knowledge. Track record is weighted heavily—founders with prior success at other startups or in relevant industries are preferred. VCs also value coachability and adaptability, seeking founders willing to take feedback and adjust strategy. Educational pedigree matters but less than track record and coachability. Interestingly, VCs recognize significant uncertainty in team assessment—many acknowledge limited ability to predict founder success ex-ante. VCs partially address this uncertainty through post-investme

coles-2008.title: Boards: Does One Size Fit All?
coles-2008.summaryPlain: Core Question Coles, Daniel, and Naveen challenge the prevailing corporate governance doctrine that smaller boards universally maximize firm value. The authors ask a deceptively simple but consequential question: is there an optimal board size, and does it vary systematically with firm characteristics? Their core claim directly contradicts the dominant governance narrative promoted by proxy advisors and reform advocates: board size should be contingent on organizational complexity, measured through diversification, leverage, and R&D intensity. This complexity-contingent view fundamentally reframes the board size debate from "smaller is better" to "optimal size depends on firm context." Theoretical Framework The paper relies on agency theory and organizational economics, integrating two opposing forces. Complex firms (conglomerates, multi-segment operations, high leverage) face greater agency costs from managing disparate business units and monitoring risk across segments. Monitoring requires expertise in multiple domains. Assumes larger boards provide superior monitoring of complex operations and coordination across divisions, but suffer increasing coordination costs and reduced decision-making speed as size grows. The framework predicts an inverted-U relationship between board size and firm value, with the optimal peak varying by firm complexity. Simple, focused firms should minimize boards for agility and rapid decision-making; diversified firms should maximize boards for comprehensive oversight and representation of stakeholder interests. This theoretical framework extends Williamson's organizational economics to governance: transaction costs of coordination increase nonlinearly with board size, while monitoring benefits increase sublinearly. Data & Methodology Sample covers approximately 1,650 US firms observed annually 1996-2003, yielding ~13,200 firm-year observations from COMPUSTAT, EXECUCOMP, and hand-collected proxy data. Uses Herfindahl index of segment re

adams-ferreira-2009.title: Women in the Boardroom
adams-ferreira-2009.summaryPlain: Core Question Adams and Ferreira investigate whether female directors enhance corporate governance and firm value, or whether gender diversity imposes monitoring costs through over-vigilance. They ask: do women in the boardroom improve governance outcomes, and if so, at what cost? The paper's critical innovation is recognizing potentially nonmonotonic effects—benefits in poorly-governed firms may become costs in well-governed ones, suggesting an optimal level of board monitoring intensity that varies with existing governance quality. This heterogeneous effects framework directly challenges universal diversity mandates that dominated governance reform in the 2010s. Theoretical Framework Two competing frameworks operate simultaneously. First, the monitoring enhancement hypothesis: women directors contribute distinct values—greater risk aversion, collaborative decision-making, stronger commitment to oversight and process integrity—improving governance quality. This hypothesis predicts female directors enhance value across all firm types. Second, the over-monitoring hypothesis: assumes there exists an optimal monitoring intensity; excessive monitoring imposes coordination costs (longer meetings, slower decisions), constrains management's strategic flexibility, and reduces firm value—effects particularly costly in well-governed firms already optimally monitored. The paper tests both mechanisms through reduced-form evidence on board meeting frequency, CEO compensation levels, and firm value (Tobin's Q). This framework extends agency theory to include monitoring cost functions: more monitoring is beneficial up to a point, beyond which costs exceed benefits. Data & Methodology Sample includes S&P 500 and S&P MidCap 400 firms 1996-2003, with ~1,400 firms and ~11,200 firm-year observations. Female board representation measured as fraction of female directors (0-60% in sample). Quasi-natural experiment exploiting exogenous shocks to female representation (state-level laws, ins

guernsey-2025.title: Thirty Years of Change: The Evolution of Classified Boards
guernsey-2025.summaryPlain: Core Question Guernsey, Guo, Liu, and Serfling document a dramatic institutional shift in US corporate governance: the decline of classified (staggered) boards from approximately 60% of firms in the 1990s to 30% by 2023—a 27.5 percentage point secular decline over 28 years. The authors investigate what explains this transformation: shareholder activism, ISS proxy advisor influence, or genuine belief that staggered boards destroy value? Critically, they adjudicate between two competing theories with opposite implications: the entrenchment hypothesis (staggered boards harm value by reducing takeover threat) versus the commitment device hypothesis (staggered boards benefit value by protecting long-term investment). Evidence for one theory has opposite governance implications than the other. Theoretical Framework Two theories predict opposite effects on firm value and governance. Entrenchment hypothesis: staggered boards reduce takeover likelihood and discipline, weakening external governance constraints and allowing managers to pursue private benefits, thereby harming firm value and accounting performance. Classified boards require replacement of ~1/3 of directors annually, reducing hostile takeover feasibility (takes two years to replace full board). Commitment device hypothesis: staggered boards protect long-term investment from myopic market pressure and short-termist shareholders; classified boards enable CEOs to pursue high-R&D, long-payoff investments without fear of hostile takeover during investment period. Evidence structure exploits that declassification effects should differ by firm type: entrenchment prediction is uniform value loss across all firms; commitment device predicts value loss concentrated in R&D-intensive, long-project firms where myopic pressure is most costly. Data & Methodology Comprehensive panel of ~3,500 firms with 28 years of observations (1995-2023), yielding ~98,000 firm-year observations. Board classification status from RiskMetrics da

dyck-2019.title: Do Institutional Investors Drive CSR?
dyck-2019.summaryPlain: Core Question Dyck, Lins, Roth, and Wagner investigate whether institutional investors actively drive corporate social responsibility and ESG (environmental, social, governance) improvements globally, or whether they simply screen to select already-compliant firms. They ask: is institutional investor ESG influence causal (active engagement moving firms to improve ESG) or merely selective (institutional investors preferring firms that already have high ESG)? This distinction is critical because it determines whether institutional investors are genuine ESG champions driving firm-level improvements or passive capital allocators sorting among existing firms. The paper employs a clever natural experiment to isolate causality: Russell index reconstitution, which exogenously changes institutional investor demand at a specific market cap threshold. Theoretical Framework Two competing mechanisms: (1) Engagement hypothesis: institutional investors use voice (shareholder proposals, voting pressure, management meetings) and exit threats (portfolio reallocation) to pressure firms to improve ESG practices, particularly environmental and social metrics. Active engagement improves firm ESG through direct management pressure. (2) Screening hypothesis: institutional investors prefer already-compliant firms; causality runs from ESG performance to institutional ownership (selection), not vice versa. If screening dominates, ESG scores should already be higher in Russell 1000 firms pre-inclusion. Assumes different institutional investor types have heterogeneous engagement capabilities and incentives: long-horizon investors (pension funds, university endowments) strongly motivated to engage and capable of firm-specific engagement; passive investors (index funds) lack incentives for firm-specific engagement and primarily track indices. The paper's key innovation: using Russell index reconstitution as natural experiment to test engagement versus screening mechanisms. Data & Methodology Natu

barber-2021.title: Impact Investing
barber-2021.summaryPlain: Core Question Barber, Morse, and Yasuda investigate the financial returns sacrifice required to achieve social and environmental impact in venture capital and private equity investing. They ask: do impact-focused VC and PE funds accept significantly lower financial returns compared to traditional funds, and do they actually achieve stated impact goals? The paper reveals a critical and troubling puzzle: many impact funds claim to deliver market-rate returns while delivering minimal incremental impact—suggesting "impact" labels without authentic commitment. Only concessionary-return funds (explicitly accepting below-market returns) deliver authentic impact, raising questions about the vast growth in ESG labeling without return concessions. Theoretical Framework Assumes impact-generating investments inherently offer lower financial returns due to competing optimization objectives or targeting capital-constrained and higher-risk populations (frontier markets, social enterprises). The fundamental tension: market-rate investors optimize returns; impact investors optimize social outcomes—simultaneously optimizing both is impossible when the two objectives trade off. Market-rate impact funds face an unsolvable dual-objective problem: they cannot simultaneously achieve market-rate returns and significant impact because truly impact-generating investments target inefficient markets where returns are depressed (frontier markets, social enterprises, climate solutions). Concessionary-return funds explicitly accept explicit return tradeoff, aligning incentive structures with impact objectives. This framework predicts mission drift in market-rate funds (converging toward traditional fund characteristics over time as pressure to meet return targets mounts) and authentic impact delivery in concessionary-return funds. Data & Methodology Comprehensive sample of 1,395 VC and PE funds raised 1991-2014, combining commercial PREQIN database (industry-standard source for fund data) with ha

edmans-2023.title: CEO Compensation: Evidence from the Field
edmans-2023.summaryPlain: Core Question Edmans, Gosling, and Jenter investigate a critical and consequential disconnect between academic theory and corporate practice: why do observed CEO compensation practices diverge so dramatically from optimal contracting theories that dominate finance research? Theory predicts compensation optimized for incentive alignment, clawback provisions enforcing accountability, and performance targets calibrated for maximum incentive power. Practice reveals different logic: peer benchmarking inertia, regulatory compliance pressure, and director/consultant liability concerns dominating. Rather than relying on archival data analysis and econometric inference to infer mechanisms (standard approach), the authors conduct field research: interviews with 46 board directors, surveys of 216 compensation consultants, and detailed case studies of 5 major companies' compensation-setting processes (2010-2014). Theoretical Framework & Practice Disconnect Academic theory emphasizes optimal contracting as foundation of CEO pay design: compensation should maximize incentive alignment (pay-for-performance), incorporate clawback provisions to enforce accountability for misconduct, avoid paying for uncontrollable luck, and use stretch targets to motivate superior performance. This theoretical framework assumes compensation committees operate to optimize contracting terms. Practice operates under different constraints and objectives: peer benchmarking inertia (firms use peer medians as anchors, reducing deliberative optimization), regulatory compliance (tax code rules on deductibility, accounting treatment, disclosure complexity), and director/consultant liability concerns (avoiding controversial compensation structures that might draw shareholder criticism) trump pure incentive optimization. The framework predicts observed pay structures reflect regulatory requirements, agency costs of managing director/consultant risk, and behavioral inertia rather than optimal incentive design. D

giglio-2025.title: Four Facts about ESG Beliefs and Investor Portfolios
giglio-2025.summaryPlain: Core Question Giglio, Maggiori, Stroebel, Tan, and Utkus survey 5,009 Vanguard retail and institutional investors to understand a central puzzle: why do investors voluntarily allocate substantial capital to ESG (environmental, social, governance) assets despite explicitly believing ESG investments earn lower financial returns than non-ESG alternatives? This directly contradicts rational asset pricing models where agents should maximize returns subject to constraints. The paper documents four striking facts that collectively demolish the rational investor model of ESG allocation and instead suggest ESG investing driven by nonpecuniary preferences (values, identity, social responsibility) rather than financial return expectations. Theoretical Framework & Motivation Rational asset pricing predicts investors should hold higher-return assets to maximize expected utility. Two explanations could reconcile ESG holding with lower-return expectations: (1) ESG reflects lower fundamental risk (decreases required return), or (2) ESG investors face external constraints (regulations, mandates requiring ESG). The paper tests both mechanisms and finds evidence supporting neither: ESG investors knowingly accept lower returns without external constraints, suggesting ESG allocations driven by nonpecuniary motives (values, identity, social preferences). Alternative framework: taste-based preferences for ESG generate demand curves independent of return expectations, creating ESG premiums driven by preference heterogeneity rather than risk or fundamentals. This has profound implications: if ESG pricing reflects tastes rather than fundamentals, ESG valuations may face reversal if investor preferences shift—a timing risk not captured in traditional asset pricing models. Data & Methodology: Large-Scale Survey with Randomized Treatment Large-scale survey of 5,009 Vanguard investors (stratified sample of retail and institutional clients) regarding (1) beliefs about ESG returns (elicited via pr

mikkelson-1986.title: Valuation Effects of Security Offerings
mikkelson-1986.summaryPlain: Core Question Do security type announcements reveal managerial private information about firm value? Mikkelson and Partch (1986) test Myers-Majluf (1984) pecking order theory by examining whether equity offerings signal overvaluation relative to debt offerings, using announcement-period cumulative abnormal returns (CARs) as evidence of information content. Theoretical Framework Myers-Majluf pecking order model: managers possess private information about firm value; when managers perceive firm as undervalued, they prefer debt/equity financing; when overvalued, equity financing signals overvaluation (and loss of control over firm to new shareholders). Security choice thus becomes a costly signal of private value estimates. Under information asymmetry (Akerlof, 1970), exterior investors discount equity offerings more than debt, creating rational negative price reactions. The magnitude of negative reaction should correlate with information asymmetry (firm size, R&D intensity, analyst coverage all proxy for asymmetry). Data & Methodology Event study of 632 security offerings announced 1980-1983 on NYSE/AMEX: 213 equity offerings (33.7%), 163 convertible debt (25.8%), 256 straight debt (40.5%). Standard market model event study with 120-day pre-event estimation window, 3-day event window [-1, 0, +1], CRSP equally-weighted market index. CARs = Σ(R_it - [α_i + β_i·R_mt]) over 3-day window. Sample restriction: offerings ≥$5M proceeds, complete return data in CRSP. Firm characteristics sourced from Compustat; analyst coverage from I/B/E/S. Key Findings Equity offerings generate mean CAR = -3.56% (t = -6.28, p<0.001), convertible debt = -2.31% (t = -4.42), straight debt = -0.26% (t = -0.67, NS). This monotonic ordering perfectly aligns with pecking order predictions: security choice reveals information, with equity signaling strongest negative message. Cross-sectional analysis: small firms' equity offering CARs = -4.8% vs. large firms -2.2% (2.6pp differential, p<0.0

loughran-1995.title: The New Issues Puzzle
loughran-1995.summaryPlain: Core Question Do firms successfully time equity issuances to exploit temporary market overvaluation? Loughran and Ritter (1995) document a striking empirical puzzle: firms that issue equity significantly underperform non-issuing peers over 5-year horizons, suggesting issuers exploit windows of overvaluation. This challenges market efficiency and complements Mikkelson-Partch's announcement-period findings with long-run persistence evidence. Theoretical Framework Market timing hypothesis: firms possess private information about value; when managers perceive market as overvalued (relative to firm's intrinsic value), they issue equity to capture excess valuations. Subsequent market repricing to fundamentals generates negative long-run returns for issuers. Alternatively, issuers may have pessimistic private information about future earnings, inducing equity issuance to avoid debt burden; negative returns then reflect deteriorating business prospects known only to insiders. Under rational expectations, both mechanisms generate systematic underperformance of issuers relative to non-issuers in the post-issuance period. Data & Methodology Buy-and-hold abnormal returns (BHARs) methodology: investor purchases issuer stock at offering price, holds 3 or 5 years, comparing cumulative return to matched control firm (non-issuer, same SIC code, similar size and book-to-market). BHAR = [(1+R_1)·(1+R_2)·...·(1+R_T)] - [(1+R_c1)·(1+R_c2)·...·(1+R_cT)], measured monthly. Sample: 4,753 IPOs (1970-1990) and 3,702 seasoned equity offerings (1970-1990), source: Ritter IPO database, CRSP, SDC. Control firm selection: 2-digit SIC code match, book-to-market ±30%, size within 70-130% of issuer. Hot vs. cold market defined by IPO volume quintiles. Key Findings IPO 5-year cumulative BHAR = -35% to -40% (annualized -7%), SEO BHAR = -40% to -45% (annualized -8%). Hot-market IPOs (issuance quintile 5) underperform by 9.2% annually vs. cold-market IPOs (quintile 1) at -4.8%, a 4.4pp differential

ljungqvist-2006.title: Competing for Securities Underwriting Mandates
ljungqvist-2006.summaryPlain: Core Question Do investment banks use analyst coverage as currency to win underwriting mandates, creating conflicts of interest? Ljungqvist, Marston, and Wilhelm (2006) provide direct evidence that analyst quality ("All-Star" status) influences underwriting mandate success and that post-mandate analyst coverage becomes systematically biased toward underwriting clients, compromising research integrity. Theoretical Framework Conflict of interest model: investment banks generate revenue from both research (subscription fees, research trading fees) and underwriting (lead manager spreads, advisory fees). When research and underwriting divisions operate within same firm, underwriting division creates incentives for research division to provide coverage favorable to underwriting clients (both pre-mandate during "pitch" phase and post-mandate as ongoing service). This biased coverage attracts institutional investor attention and facilitates equity issuance, boosting underwriting success rates and profitability. Analyst recommendations and earnings forecasts reflect not just fundamentals but also client relationships, creating information degradation for external investors. Data & Methodology Comprehensive dataset linking 16,625 offerings (1993-2002, IPOs and SEOs) to analyst coverage (I/B/E/S), recommendations, and earnings forecasts. Identification of lead/co-lead underwriters from SDC (Securities Data Corporation). For each offering, researchers track analyst coverage by underwriter-affiliated analysts in 12 months before and after announcement. All-Star analysts identified from Institutional Investor magazine polls (annually ranks top analysts by sector). Analyst coverage change calculated as difference in number of analyzing analysts pre vs. post offering. Recommendation bias measured as fraction of buy recommendations relative to independent (non-underwriter) analyst coverage of comparable firms. Key Findings Lead underwriters with All-Star analysts win 15-20% high

celikurt-2010.title: Going Public to Acquire?
celikurt-2010.summaryPlain: Core Question Do IPO firms pursue acquisitions within 3 years of going public? Celikurt (2010) examines whether equity issuance serves as acquisition currency and whether IPO-acquirers experience different M&A announcement returns and post-acquisition performance compared to seasoned acquirers. This research connects IPO outcomes to corporate strategy, examining whether access to public equity markets enables growth by acquisition. Theoretical Framework Overvaluation hypothesis for acquisitions: when firms go public at inflated valuations (consistent with Loughran-Ritter 1995), inflated equity price provides currency for acquisitions; acquirer uses overvalued stock to purchase target firm at discounted valuation relative to fundamentals, transferring value from acquirer shareholders to target shareholders. IPO acquirers therefore should experience negative announcement returns (value destruction) more than seasoned acquirers. Alternative hypothesis: IPO provides access to capital constraints; acquisitions by previously capital-constrained IPO firms represent value-enhancing growth investments, generating positive returns. Empirically, net effect depends on relative magnitudes of overvaluation and growth option hypotheses. Data & Methodology Sample of 3,144 IPOs (1980-2004) from Ritter database; identify subset executing acquisition within 36 months post-IPO (n=687, 21.8%). Acquisition events sourced from SDC (Securities Data Corporation) and SEC filings (8-K acquisition announcements). M&A announcement returns computed via standard event study: 3-day cumulative abnormal returns (CARs) using market model (120-day pre-event estimation window). Matched-sample comparison: for each IPO-acquirer, identify seasoned acquirer (public ≥5 years, similar size, same 2-digit SIC) that completed acquisition in same year; compare announcement returns and subsequent performance across matched pairs. Key Findings IPO-acquirers experience negative M&A announcement returns:

liu-2011.title: Local Underwriter Oligopolies and IPO Underpricing
liu-2011.summaryPlain: Core Question Does geographic concentration of underwriters (oligopolistic market structure) affect IPO underpricing and gross spreads? Liu (2011) investigates whether underwriter market power, measured via Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) of market share, explains 5-15% variation in IPO underpricing across markets. This extends beyond Mikkelson-Partch (1986) information-based explanations to examine competitive market structure effects. Theoretical Framework Underwriter market power hypothesis: in concentrated (high-HHI) markets, lead underwriter faces less competition from rivals, enabling rent extraction via higher gross spreads and reduced pricing discipline, leading to excessive underpricing. In competitive (low-HHI) markets, underwriter margins compressed, creating incentives for aggressive pricing to win mandates, reducing underpricing (or maintaining consistent pricing for reputation benefits). Underpricing = (offer price - post-IPO trading price) / offer price, represents compensation to underwriter for price stabilization and information acquisition services. Competition policy implications: antitrust concerns if underwriter concentration demonstrably harms issuers via reduced pricing discipline. Data & Methodology IPO sample: 4,286 US IPOs (1980-2005), sourced from Ritter database and SDC. Underwriter identification from prospectuses; lead and co-lead underwriter gross spreads from SEC filings. IPO underpricing = (first-day closing price - offer price) / offer price, computed from CRSP daily prices (first trade post-listing). Market structure measured via Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) computed from underwriter market shares (by proceeds volume) in IPO market, calculated annually and geographically (state-level and metropolitan statistical area level). Cross-sectional regression: underpricing on HHI with controls for firm risk, information asymmetry, and offering characteristics (standard approach following Ritter 2003). fernando-2012.title: Value of Investment Banking Relationships: Lehman Collapse
fernando-2012.summaryPlain: Core Question Do investment banking relationships have economic value? Fernando and Limpaphayom (2012) use the Lehman Brothers collapse (September 2008) as a natural experiment to examine relationship-specific capital in banking. Lehman's sudden failure forced client firms to switch underwriters, enabling estimation of switching costs and relationship value via abnormal returns around relationship termination. Theoretical Framework Relationship capital model: investment banks accumulate relationship-specific capital with issuer clients (firm-specific knowledge, trust, deal networks, government connections). This relationship capital generates quasi-rents (monopolistic pricing, continued mandates). When bank fails and relationship terminates, issuer must incur switching costs (time to develop new banking relationship, temporary information disadvantage, bargaining power loss). The capitalized value of relationship-specific capital appears in switching costs. Lehman collapse provides exogenous termination: Lehman-connected firms' abnormal returns at collapse measure lost relationship value (destroyed quasi-rents). Data & Methodology Sample: firms with investment banking relationships to Lehman Brothers (identified from SEC filings: 8-K forms listing Lehman as underwriter for prior offerings in 5-year window, or having Lehman involvement in 5-year M&A/advisory activity). Event date: September 15, 2008 (Lehman bankruptcy filing). Comparison group: firms with relationships to other large investment banks (not in immediate distress at Sept 2008). Event study: cumulative abnormal returns (CARs) for Lehman-connected firms around September 15, 2008 (event window: -2 to +10 trading days), estimated relative to control group non-Lehman firms. Long-run abnormal returns measured via buy-and-hold methodology over 6-month and 12-month windows post-bankruptcy. Key Findings Lehman-connected firms experience significantly negative abnormal returns at Lehman's collapse: 3-day CAR

bernstein-2015.title: Does Going Public Affect Innovation?
bernstein-2015.summaryPlain: Core Question Does going public diminish firm innovation? Bernstein (2015) uses regression discontinuity design (NASDAQ listing threshold) to estimate causal effect of IPO on patent quality, inventor retention, and firm innovation trajectory. This addresses long-standing question of whether access to public capital markets incentivizes short-termism, reducing exploratory innovation. Theoretical Framework Short-termism hypothesis: public equity markets pressure managers to meet quarterly earnings targets (quarterly reporting requirements, analyst expectations), creating incentive to shift from exploratory innovation (long-term, uncertain payoff) to exploitation (short-term profit maximization). Alternatively, public markets provide capital access and diversification (public shareholders vs. concentrated VC ownership), potentially increasing innovation. Causal identification requires exogenous variation in IPO decision independent of firm innovation prospects. NASDAQ listing rule (automatic listing upon meeting trading volume threshold) provides such variation: firms barely above threshold (forced to list) can be compared to just-below threshold (can remain private). Data & Methodology Regression discontinuity (RD) using NASDAQ listing threshold: firms with trading volume exceeding 100,000 shares/day threshold automatically qualify for listing; firms below must stay private or seek alternative listing. This creates discontinuity in IPO likelihood around threshold. Patent data from USPTO; inventor names matched to firm using patent assignee information. Dependent variables: (1) patent quality, measured as future patent citations (citations per patent, indicator of value/importance), normalized by technology class and cohort; (2) inventor turnover (percentage of inventors who change firms within 3 years post-IPO); (3) patent innovation type (classification into exploratory vs. exploitation based on backward/forward citations and technology proximity). Key Findings G

doidge-2017.title: The U.S. Listing Gap
doidge-2017.summaryPlain: Core Question Why has the number of US-listed firms declined 50% since 1996? Doidge, Karolyi, and Stulz (2017) document secular decline in listed firm count (5,996 firms in 1996 to 3,671 in 2012) and attribute it to regulatory burden (Sarbanes-Oxley 2002, increased compliance costs), reduced gains from going public, and rise of private equity as alternative to public listing. This addresses IPO supply-side determinants complementing demand-side analysis (Loughran 1995 long-run returns). Theoretical Framework Regulatory burden hypothesis: Sarbanes-Oxley (2002) imposed costly corporate governance reforms (independent audit committees, auditor attestation of internal controls, Dodd-Frank amendments 2010-2012 expanded costs). These fixed costs disproportionately burden small firms, raising IPO break-even firm size threshold. Consequently, smaller potential IPO candidates remain private or pursue private equity buyouts instead. Alternative hypotheses: (1) economic fundamentals (reduced growth opportunities, lower firm profitability), (2) demand side (reduced investor appetite for small-cap equity, index inclusion preferences), (3) technological change (reduced role of public markets in enabling capital formation given private venture capital scale). Causal identification via cross-country comparisons: US regulatory intensity higher than international peers; if SOX causal, US firm count decline should exceed other countries. Data & Methodology Analysis combines: (1) firm count data from CRSP (1970-2014), decomposing exits via IPOs, mergers, delistings, privatizations; (2) firm-level characteristics (size, profitability, growth) comparing listing-eligible (threshold potential) with actual listing decisions; (3) international comparison: firm count trends in Canada, UK, Germany, Japan controlling for economic development and firm population changes. p-diamond-1984.title: Financial Intermediation and Delegated Monitoring
p-diamond-1984.summaryPlain: Introduction to Delegated Monitoring Diamond's seminal 1984 paper establishes the theoretical foundation for understanding financial intermediation as a solution to monitoring problems in credit markets. The paper addresses a fundamental question in financial economics: why do banks exist as specialized financial institutions rather than individuals lending directly to borrowers? Diamond argues that banks serve a crucial economic function by acting as delegated monitors. In environments where individual lenders lack the expertise, resources, or incentives to monitor borrowers directly, banks emerge as specialized institutions that can efficiently monitor on behalf of many depositors. This framework transforms our understanding of banking from a simple passthrough mechanism to a sophisticated information processor. The delegated monitoring hypothesis suggests that the monitoring problem is not inherent to the borrowing-lending relationship itself, but rather stems from the asymmetric information and high costs associated with individual monitoring efforts. By centralizing monitoring at the institutional level, banks can reduce these costs significantly and improve the overall efficiency of credit allocation in the economy. Diversification and the Elimination of Duplication A key insight of Diamond's work is that diversification of loan portfolios leads to the elimination of duplicative monitoring efforts. When a bank holds a diversified portfolio of loans from many borrowers, the bank need only monitor each borrower once on behalf of all its depositors. This stands in stark contrast to a scenario where each individual depositor must independently monitor each borrower in which they invest. The duplication problem is particularly severe when multiple lenders independently monitor the same borrower, leading to wasteful redundancy in monitoring resources. p-hochberg-2007.title: VC Networks and Investment Performance
p-hochberg-2007.authors: Hochberg, Yael; Ljungqvist, Alexander; Lu, Yang
p-hochberg-2007.summaryPlain: Network Analysis in Venture Capital Hochberg, Ljungqvist, and Lu (2007) conduct a comprehensive empirical investigation into the relationship between venture capital fund managers' network position and subsequent investment performance. Their study covers 3,469 venture capital funds investing between 1980 and 1999, examining how network centrality—a measure of a fund manager's position within the network of syndication relationships—predicts fund returns. The paper applies graph-theoretic measures to quantify network position, moving beyond traditional performance analysis to incorporate relational data from syndication partnerships. The core finding is that funds managed by more central network players achieve significantly higher returns, suggesting that network position is an economically important predictor of venture capital success. This research bridges the gap between social network analysis and finance, demonstrating that the pattern and intensity of professional relationships have measurable impacts on investment outcomes. The paper establishes that venture capital is not simply a matter of individual fund quality but involves important network effects where successful funds benefit from their position within the broader venture capital ecosystem. Graph-Theoretic Measures of Network Centrality A key methodological contribution of the paper is the application of graph-theoretic centrality measures to the venture capital syndication network. The authors construct a network where nodes represent individual venture capital funds and edges represent syndication partnerships—instances where two funds co-invest in the same company. Centrality measures quantify a fund's position within this network structure. p-sufi-2007.title: Information Asymmetry and Syndicated Loans
p-sufi-2007.summaryPlain: Introduction to Information Asymmetry in Loan Markets Amir Sufi's 2007 paper examines how borrower transparency affects the syndication structure of bank loans. Syndication—the process whereby multiple lenders share a single loan to a borrower—is a ubiquitous feature of corporate lending markets, particularly for large loans. Sufi investigates a fundamental question: does the degree of information asymmetry between lenders and borrowers affect how loans are divided among syndicate members? His analysis demonstrates a striking empirical regularity: lead arrangers retain larger portions of loans made to opaque borrowers, while transparent borrowers are financed through more diffuse syndicates where loan participation is spread more evenly among lenders. This pattern suggests that syndication is not merely a mechanism for risk sharing but also a monitoring arrangement where lead arrangers take larger positions in information-sensitive loans to motivate careful monitoring. The paper bridges information economics and the structure of financial markets, showing how asymmetric information shapes institutional design and contractual arrangements. The findings have important implications for understanding credit market function, bank behavior, and the role of financial intermediation in channeling capital to different types of borrowers. Lead Arranger Retention and Monitoring Incentives A core insight of Sufi's analysis is that lead arrangers' loan retention decisions reflect their monitoring function. When a borrower is opaque—meaning that financial information about the borrower is limited, unaudited, or difficult for outsiders to interpret—the lead arranger faces greater difficulty assessing credit quality and monitoring loan performance. In such cases, the lead arranger has stronger incentives to retain a larger share of the loan, maintaining skin in the game. By retaining a larger position, the lead arranger internalizes more of the loan's risk, creating stronger incent

p-acharya-2011.title: Creditor Rights and Corporate Risk-Taking
p-acharya-2011.authors: Acharya, Viral V.; Amihud, Yakov; Litov, Luboš
p-acharya-2011.summaryPlain: Introduction to Creditor Rights and Corporate Behavior Acharya, Amihud, and Litov (2011) examine how the legal protection afforded to creditors affects corporate risk-taking and investment decisions. Using data from 39 countries with varying creditor protection regimes, the authors document that firms in countries with stronger creditor rights undertake less risky projects and maintain more diversified business portfolios. This paper contributes to the growing literature on how legal institutions shape financial behavior, demonstrating that creditor rights are not merely technical legal provisions but have substantial economic consequences for firm strategy. The research employs a natural experiment framework, comparing firms in different legal regimes to isolate the causal effect of creditor rights. By examining multiple countries with different legal traditions, the paper provides robust evidence that institutional design matters for corporate decision-making. The findings have implications for understanding how creditor protections influence the risk-return tradeoff that firms face, how leverage and risk-taking are related, and how legal systems shape the allocation of capital across risky and safe projects. Theoretical Framework: Creditor Rights and Agency Costs The paper builds on agency theory to develop predictions about how creditor rights affect corporate risk-taking. When creditor rights are strong, creditors can monitor firms more effectively, restrict dividends, and take control in cases of financial distress. These protections reduce the risk of loss to creditors and align managerial incentives with creditor interests. In contrast, when creditor rights are weak, creditors have limited ability to constrain managerial behavior, and managers have greater latitude to pursue high-risk, high-reward projects. From a manager's perspective, borrowing at favorable terms when creditor rights are weak provides a subsidy to risk-taking: creditors, unable to monitor

p-puri-2012.title: VC vs Non-VC Firms Life Cycle
p-puri-2012.summaryPlain: Introduction to VC-Backed Firm Performance Puri and Zarutskie (2012) conduct a comprehensive empirical investigation using U.S. Census data to compare the life-cycle trajectories of venture capital-backed firms versus non-VC-backed firms. Using matched sample methodology and quasi-experimental techniques, the authors document that VC-backed firms experience substantially higher employment growth and lower failure rates compared to matched non-VC firms. These findings address a fundamental question about the value of venture capital: does the dramatically higher valuation and funding provided by VC investors translate into superior real outcomes for firms? The paper's use of Census data—which covers the entire population of U.S. firms including private firms—overcomes a major limitation of prior research that relies on public company data or VC-specific databases. The Census data allows comparison of VC-backed firms with similar non-VC-backed firms on the basis of initial characteristics, size, and industry. The paper carefully distinguishes between selection effects (VC investors pick better firms) and treatment effects (VC investors add value to their portfolio firms), providing insight into whether VC funding is causally beneficial or merely correlates with firm quality. Census Data and Matched Sample Methodology A major contribution of the paper is its use of U.S. Census Bureau data to construct a comprehensive sample of VC-backed and non-VC-backed firms. The Census Bureau's Longitudinal Business Database tracks all U.S. private firms with employees and enables researchers to follow firms from founding through various outcomes including growth, survival, acquisition, or failure. The authors use census data on firm employment, industry, location, and founding date to identify firms and track their outcomes over time. The sample of VC-backed firms is constructed by matching Census records with data from Thompson Financial, which maintains a comprehensive database o

p-gompers-2020.title: How Do VCs Make Decisions?
p-gompers-2020.authors: Gompers, Paul A.; Gornall, Will; Kaplan, Steven N.; Strebulaev, Ilya A.
p-gompers-2020.summaryPlain: Introduction to VC Decision-Making Gompers, Gornall, Kaplan, and Strebulaev (2020) conduct a comprehensive survey of 885 venture capital professionals to understand the investment decision-making process in the venture capital industry. The survey covers major venture capital firms across the United States and gathers detailed information about how VC partners evaluate investment opportunities, what factors they consider most important, and how their decision-making processes have evolved over time. This paper provides unprecedented empirical evidence about the inner workings of venture capital investment, moving beyond historical analysis of VC returns or portfolio company characteristics to understand the actual decision-making frameworks employed by VC investors. The research reveals that VC decision-making is multifaceted and nuanced: while financial metrics and market analysis play important roles, VC investors place heavy emphasis on qualitative factors including team quality, founder characteristics, and product-market fit. The survey approach provides a window into the subjective judgments and heuristics that venture capitalists employ when selecting investments. The paper has important implications for understanding venture capital effectiveness, the sources of VC performance differences, and the nature of information intermediation in venture capital markets. Survey Design and Sample of 885 VCs A major strength of the paper is the scope and comprehensiveness of the survey instrument. The authors surveyed 885 individual venture capital professionals, representing a large fraction of the active VC community in the United States. The survey was conducted between 2011-2014, covering the post-financial crisis period and the early mobile/cloud computing era. The survey respondents include partners and principals at VC firms across the full range of firm sizes and stages, from early-stage seed investors to late-stage growth investors. The sample is sufficiently l

acw2004.title: Cash Flow Sensitivity of Cash
acw2004.summaryPlain: Cash Savings Behavior of Financially Constrained Firms Almeida, Campello, and Weisbach (2004) investigate whether firms facing financial constraints display fundamentally different cash management strategies compared to unconstrained firms. Using a comprehensive sample of 1,973 firms from Compustat spanning 1971-2000, they find that financially constrained firms save approximately $0.05-0.06 of each marginal dollar of cash flow as additional cash balances , while unconstrained firms show zero sensitivity (cash-to-cash flow correlation statistically indistinguishable from zero). The difference is economically and statistically significant, indicating that financial constraints fundamentally alter optimal cash management. Constrained firms face higher external financing costs when accessing debt or equity markets, making internal cash accumulation relatively cheaper than external financing for future investment . Unconstrained firms with ready access to capital markets at reasonable costs have no incentive to accumulate cash from operations, instead distributing excess cash through dividends and buybacks. Cash Flow Sensitivity Classification Systems The paper employs three distinct methodologies to classify firms as financially constrained or unconstrained, enabling cross-validation and testing robustness of findings to classification methodology. The Kaplan-Zingales (KZ) index combines five firm characteristics (cash flow, cash, leverage, divided dummy, and cash conversion cycle) via logit analysis estimated from a sample of firms with documented financing decisions. The KZ index ranges from highly constrained (negative values) to unconstrained (positive values), with prior research indicating that negative values predict constrained classification. The Whited-Wu (WW) index extends KZ with additional variables including asset tangibility, real estate holdings, and dividend policy , designed to improve constrained firm classification by capturing collateral value and

psw2006.title: Cash Holdings Value and Governance
psw2006.summaryPlain: Valuation of Cash Across Governance Regimes This groundbreaking study examines how corporate governance quality affects the value that markets place on corporate cash holdings. Using a cross-country dataset spanning multiple nations with varying levels of investor protection and governance quality, the authors apply the Faulkender-Wang methodology to estimate the marginal value of an additional dollar of cash. The central finding is striking: in countries with strong governance and high investor protection (e.g., United States, United Kingdom), an additional dollar of cash is valued at approximately $0.91—a discount of only 9 cents. In contrast, in countries with weak governance and poor investor protection (e.g., some emerging markets), the same dollar of cash is valued at only $0.33—a discount of 67 cents. This dramatic difference reveals that governance quality fundamentally shapes how much shareholders benefit from corporate cash holdings. Agency Costs and Cash Diversion The authors attribute the valuation gap to agency costs associated with cash holdings. In poorly governed firms, managers and controlling shareholders have greater ability and incentive to misappropriate cash for private benefits—excess compensation, empire-building acquisitions, related-party transactions, or direct theft. Cash is the most liquid asset and therefore most vulnerable to expropriation. Shareholders in poorly governed countries rationally discount the value of cash, understanding that much of it may never generate shareholder value. The 67-cent valuation in weak governance countries implies that shareholders expect approximately two-thirds of any marginal dollar in cash to be diverted to managerial uses. This calculation transforms our understanding of cash holdings from a liquidity asset to a potential channel for shareholder wealth extraction by insiders. Investor Protection and Contractual Enforcement The variation in cash valuation across countries correlates strongly with lega

jy2012.title: Political Uncertainty and Investment Cycles
jy2012.summaryPlain: Political Uncertainty and Corporate Investment Decisions This influential study documents a striking relationship between political uncertainty and corporate investment across 24 countries spanning developed and emerging markets. Julio and Yook find that firms significantly reduce capital expenditure during election years—a period of heightened political uncertainty about policy direction. The magnitude is substantial: aggregate investment declines by 4.8% in election years relative to non-election years. This reduction is economically significant given that aggregate capital expenditure represents a large share of GDP and corporate investment is a key driver of productivity growth and economic development. The finding challenges conventional corporate finance theory by suggesting that policy uncertainty—an environmental factor outside firm control—materially affects how much capital firms deploy in productive assets. The study identifies investment as a margin through which political uncertainty propagates into the real economy, affecting growth, employment, and productivity. Mechanisms: Irreversibility and Waiting Costs The theoretical foundation for why political uncertainty reduces investment rests on the irreversibility of capital investments combined with the option value of waiting. When firms face irreversible investment decisions (capital cannot be easily redeployed if policy changes unfavorably), uncertainty creates incentives to delay investment until the policy environment becomes clearer. gi2016.title: Policy Uncertainty and Corporate Investment
gi2016.summaryPlain: Economic Policy Uncertainty Index and Corporate Investment Gulen and Ion construct a quantitative measure of economic policy uncertainty—the Baker, Bloom and Davis (BBD) Economic Policy Uncertainty (EPU) index—and study how variation in this index predicts corporate capital expenditure decisions. The EPU index combines newspaper-based measures of policy-related economic uncertainty with forecaster disagreement and tax code expirations, capturing policy uncertainty as an observable economic variable. Using this index, the authors find that a one standard deviation increase in the EPU index is associated with a 6% decline in capital investment (measured as capital expenditure to assets ratio). This result parallels the Julio-Yook finding but uses a continuous measure of policy uncertainty rather than binary election indicators, allowing for more granular analysis. The magnitude is economically substantial: given that policy uncertainty fluctuates substantially over time (particularly spiking during financial crises, elections, and legislative uncertainty), the effect represents a meaningful channel through which policy uncertainty affects economic outcomes. Policy Uncertainty and Irreversible Investment A central theoretical contribution of Gulen and Ion is demonstrating that the sensitivity of investment to policy uncertainty increases with investment irreversibility. Firms with highly irreversible capital—manufacturing firms, utilities, firms with substantial fixed assets—show much stronger investment declines when policy uncertainty increases. In contrast, firms with more reversible or flexible capital—service firms, firms relying primarily on labor inputs, asset-light business models—show minimal investment responses to policy uncertainty. This finding directly supports real options theory: when capital cannot be costlessly redeployed if policy changes unfavorably, uncertainty creates powerful incentives to delay investment. julio_yook_2012.title: Political Uncertainty and Investment Cycles
julio_yook_2012.summaryPlain: Political Uncertainty and Corporate Investment Decisions This influential study documents a striking relationship between political uncertainty and corporate investment across 24 countries spanning developed and emerging markets. Julio and Yook find that firms significantly reduce capital expenditure during election years—a period of heightened political uncertainty about policy direction. The magnitude is substantial: aggregate investment declines by 4.8% in election years relative to non-election years. The finding challenges conventional corporate finance theory by suggesting that policy uncertainty—an environmental factor outside firm control—materially affects how much capital firms deploy in productive assets. The study identifies investment as a margin through which political uncertainty propagates into the real economy, affecting growth, employment, and productivity. gilje_taillard_2016.title: Do Private Firms Invest Differently Than Public Firms? Evidence from Exogenous Variation
gilje_taillard_2016.summaryPlain: Core Question & Motivation This paper investigates whether public and private firms make fundamentally different investment decisions, and whether observed differences reflect agency costs, capital constraints, or heterogeneous managerial preferences. The canonical view in corporate finance suggests that public firms face agency problems (dispersed shareholders, short-termist pressure from markets) driving excessive payouts and insufficient investment in long-horizon projects. Conversely, private firms should invest more in illiquid, long-duration assets due to longer decision horizons and alignment between owners and managers. Yet prior empirical work struggles to distinguish causation from selection: private and public firms differ in size, profitability, industry, and unobserved quality, confounding comparisons. This paper uses a natural experiment in the natural gas extraction industry: the construction of a natural gas pipeline that suddenly grants access to previously isolated reserves. Pipeline access is plausibly exogenous to firm characteristics, enabling causal comparison of investment responses between private and public firms facing identical new opportunity sets. The research question is crucial for understanding corporate governance: if private firms systematically invest more or differently in long-horizon projects, this suggests that public firm governance structures distort investment allocation toward short-term shareholder returns at the expense of long-term value creation. Theoretical Framework The paper leverages agency theory and real options theory to predict differential investment responses. Agency theory predicts that dispersed public shareholders create agency conflicts with managers, leading to excessive distributions (dividends, buybacks) and underinvestment in long-term capital (R&D, infrastructure, exploration). Public equity markets penalize negative earnings surprises and reward short-term earnings beats, creating incentive alignment

adam_fernando_2006.title: Hedging, Speculation and Shareholder Value
adam_fernando_2006.summaryPlain: Core Question & Motivation This paper investigates how corporate derivatives strategies create shareholder value, moving beyond the conventional wisdom that hedging simply reduces risk and therefore should increase firm value. Standard corporate finance theory posits that firms hedge to reduce distress costs, smooth cash flows, or minimize expected tax liabilities, implying that hedging reduces firm risk and therefore reduces expected returns. However, financial markets reward some firms handsomely for hedging activities, suggesting additional value creation mechanisms beyond simple risk reduction. This paper examines gold mining companies and their derivatives hedging strategies, revealing that value creation through hedging operates partly through a speculation channel: firms engage in selective hedging that creates asymmetric payoff structures, similar to buying out-of-the-money call options. The contribution is demonstrating that hedging and speculation need not be opposites; rather, a firm's derivative positions can simultaneously hedge certain risks while creating directional bets (speculation) on commodity prices. The value creation mechanism is not risk reduction per se, but rather the ability to profit from directional commodity price movements while maintaining operational flexibility. This challenges the view that all hedging is conservative risk management and suggests that how firms hedge—the structure of derivative contracts and their dynamic management—matters enormously for shareholder value. The paper ultimately argues that corporate derivatives are not just insurance but can be profit centers when managers skillfully time or forecast price movements. Theoretical Framework The theoretical framework combines three economic principles: real options theory, information asymmetry/signaling, and dynamic hedging optimization. cong_he_2019.title: Blockchain Disruption and Smart Contracts
cong_he_2019.summaryPlain: Core Question & Motivation This paper examines how smart contracts—self-executing code on blockchain platforms—reduce transaction costs by enabling automated enforcement without intermediaries, and analyzes the implications for organizational form, financial market structure, and economic efficiency. The fundamental economic problem is verification: parties to a transaction must incur costs to verify that the other party has fulfilled contractual obligations, or rely on costly intermediaries (lawyers, courts, escrow agents) to verify and enforce. Smart contracts reduce this verification cost by embedding contract logic in code that executes automatically when predefined conditions are met: if payment is received, the smart contract automatically releases the asset without requiring a third party to verify that payment actually occurred. The authors develop a theoretical framework modeling how reductions in verification costs affect firm structure, transaction patterns, and industrial organization. They argue that blockchain and smart contracts represent a fundamental technological shift comparable to the development of double-entry bookkeeping or the telegraph: they enable new organizational forms (decentralized autonomous organizations), new market structures (permissionless protocols), and new transaction types previously infeasible. The quantitative impact on verification costs is enormous: intermediation costs for simple transactions (e.g., payments, asset transfers) can decline from 1–3% to near-zero when using smart contracts, a 50–100x reduction for certain transaction types. Understanding whether and why this cost reduction translates into real economic disruption requires analyzing adoption frictions and competitive dynamics among blockchain platforms. Theoretical Framework The theoretical foundation rests on several economic principles: transaction cost economics, network effects, and real options under uncertainty. paper1.title: Performance Persistence in Entrepreneurship
paper1.summaryPlain: 1. Research Question and Motivation Why do banks combine deposit-taking and lending? This foundational question requires theoretical justification. Kashyap, Rajan, and Stein (2002) argue that the synergy arises from a fundamental characteristic: both deposits and loan commitments are liquid liabilities that require holding liquid assets to satisfy claims. The key insight is that the correlation between deposit inflows and loan commitment drawdowns is imperfect—typically negative or weakly positive. When one liability pressures the bank's liquidity position, the other often provides relief, creating economic value through natural diversification of liquidity risk. This imperfect correlation is non-trivial: deposits tend to flow in during market stress when firms draw credit lines, while normal periods see simultaneous claims on both. This natural hedge justifies why a single financial institution combines both activities rather than specializing in one. 2. Theoretical Framework and Mechanism The paper builds on human capital theory of entrepreneurship but extends it to incorporate explicit liquidity risk management. KRS develops a model where banks face two types of liquid liabilities: deposits (short-term, can withdraw on demand or have contracted withdrawal rights) and credit commitments (undrawn portions that firms can access when needed). The key theoretical prediction is that if deposits and commitment drawdowns are imperfectly correlated, banks should rationally combine them. The framework predicts that banks holding a higher proportion of transaction deposits (which provide natural liquidity inflows in certain states) should be willing to hold more illiquid loan commitments. The model generates testable implications: (1) banks with more stable deposit bases should hold more commitments; (2) the relationship should be strongest during periods of high uncertainty about future liquidity shocks; (3) regulatory changes affecting deposit access should predict change

paper2.title: With a Little Help from My (Random) Friends: Success and Failure in Post-Business School Entrepreneurship
paper2.summaryPlain: 1. Research Question and Conceptual Framework Gatev and Strahan (2006) directly test the mechanism proposed by Kashyap, Rajan, and Stein (2002): do deposits actually flow into banks when loan commitments are drawn, providing the hypothesized liquidity hedge? This is critical because the entire KRS liquidity synergy argument rests on the imperfect correlation between deposit flows and commitment drawdowns. Gatev and Strahan argue that banks have a competitive advantage in hedging liquidity risk precisely because deposits tend to increase when other financial markets experience stress, particularly when commercial paper (CP) markets tighten and firms turn to bank credit lines instead. Simultaneously, during CP market stress, households and firms may increase deposits at banks for perceived safety. If these flows are indeed negatively correlated—deposits rising when commitments are drawn—then banks can manage liquidity naturally without costly external funding, creating value. 2. Identification Strategy and Econometric Approach Gatev and Strahan employ a clever identification strategy using commercial paper market stress as an exogenous shock to loan commitment drawdowns. The key independent variable is the TED spread (3-month LIBOR minus 3-month T-Bill rate), which measures credit risk premia in short-term funding markets. During normal times, the TED spread is approximately 10-20 basis points; during stress (like the 1998 LTCM crisis or 2002 credit events), it spikes to 50-100+ basis points. The logic is that when the TED spread widens, financial markets view credit risk as elevated, and firms with bank credit lines (rather than CP market access) become more willing to draw those lines as an alternative to unreliable commercial paper funding. This creates an exogenous demand shock to banks' commitment facilities. paper3.title: Financing Innovation: Evidence from R&D Grants
paper3.summaryPlain: 1. Core Research Question and Policy Motivation Ashcraft (2005) asks a fundamental question that goes to the heart of financial regulation: beyond regulatory favoritism like deposit insurance, are banks economically special? Do they perform unique economic functions that justify their special regulatory treatment and the substantial costs of banking regulation (capital requirements, stress tests, liquidity requirements, restrictions on activities)? The question is policy-critical because if banks are not truly special—if financial intermediation could be provided equally well by other institutions (shadow banks, securities markets, peer-to-peer lending)—then regulation might be unnecessarily restrictive. Conversely, if banks provide unique services with significant positive spillovers to the real economy, regulation is justified and even economically welfare-enhancing. Ashcraft's innovative approach is to examine what happens when a healthy bank fails: does it cause economic damage beyond depositor losses, suggesting banks provide irreplaceable functions? 2. Natural Experiment Design and Identification Strategy Ashcraft uses natural experiments provided by FDIC bank failures, specifically examining two major episodes: the First City Bancorp collapse (1992) and the First Republic Bank failure (1988). Crucially, both failures involved subsidiary banks that were economically healthy (profitable, adequately capitalized) yet failed because parent companies collapsed due to concentrated commercial real estate losses—the subsidiaries themselves were not the source of weakness. This creates a natural experiment: the bank subsidiary failure was essentially exogenous to local credit conditions. The identification strategy uses a difference-in-differences (DiD) framework: Outcome_{m,t} = α + β(BankFailed_m × PostFailure_t) + Controls_{m,t} + μ_m + τ_t + ε_{m,t}, where m indexes geographic markets/counties and t indexes time periods. paper4.title: Cost of Experimentation and the Evolution of Venture Capital
paper4.summaryPlain: 1. Core Research Question How have technological shocks to startup costs transformed venture capital investment strategies? Over the past decade, the cost of starting certain types of technology companies has declined dramatically. Cloud infrastructure (AWS), open-source software, and other technological infrastructure have reduced capital requirements for experimentation. How have VCs adapted their strategies in response to these cost-of-experimentation shocks? The paper investigates whether VCs have shifted toward "spray and pray" approaches (smaller checks, lighter governance) and whether this reflects rational adaptation to lower-cost experimentation environment or suboptimal herding behavior. 2. Theoretical Framework The authors develop a theoretical framework showing how reductions in cost of starting ventures lead to optimal changes in VC strategy. With lower startup costs, the option value of trying a new venture increases while the cost decreases—this makes "thin" investments (small initial checks) with staged funding rationally optimal. VCs can fund more startups initially, gather information about relative quality/market fit, then concentrate capital in most promising ventures. The framework predicts: (1) initial funding amounts should decrease, (2) number of new ventures funded should increase or remain constant, (3) governance and follow-on investment should become more selective/staged. The paper tests whether observed VC strategy changes (smaller initial investments, less governance intensity, staged follow-on) align with predictions of cost-of-experimentation model versus alternative explanations (market saturation, talent competition, irrational herding). 3. Data & Methodology Data combines VentureSource (now Crunchbase) with hand-collected data on founder characteristics, VC governance involvement, and company outcomes. paper5.title: Uncertainty and Investment Dynamics
paper5.summaryPlain: Core Question Does hedging foreign currency exposure increase firm market value? Allayannis & Weston (2001) examine 720 non-financial U.S. firms over 1990-1995, testing whether firms using foreign currency derivatives (FCDs) command a hedging premium. This shifts focus from behavioral (managerial incentives, Tufano 1996) to fundamental shareholder value channel. Theoretical Framework Hedging Value Creation Mechanism: In perfect capital markets (Modigliani-Miller), hedging is irrelevant. With market imperfections, hedging creates value through: (1) Reduced expected financial distress costs (Froot, Scharfstein & Stein) —stable cash flows enable higher debt capacity; (2) Tax convexity (Jensen's Inequality) —hedging stabilizes taxable income, increasing expected tax payments under progressive schedules; (3) Mitigation of agency costs —reducing firm volatility constrains manager risk-shifting incentives. Empirical Prediction: Firms with material foreign currency exposure should benefit more from hedging. Hedgers' Tobin's Q should exceed non-hedgers' Q by a statistically and economically significant margin. Data & Methodology Sample: 720 non-financial firms from S&P 500, AMEX, and NASDAQ (1990-1995). Selection: firms with >10% foreign sales (identified from 10-K filings). Data sources: (1) Firm hedging activity (did firm use FCDs?) from annual reports and hedge accounting footnotes; (2) Foreign sales percentages from segment reporting; (3) Financial data (assets, debt, market cap) from COMPUSTAT; (4) Stock prices from CRSP. Key Variables: Dependent: Tobin's Q = (market cap + book debt) / book assets. paper6.title: Intangible Capital and the Investment-q Relation
paper6.summaryPlain: Core Question Does hedging increase firm value in oil and gas producers, and if so, through what mechanism? Jin & Jorion (2006) directly test the Allayannis-Weston (2001) finding of a 4.87% hedging premium in a setting with transparent, easily-measurable commodity price exposure. The oil/gas industry allows precise measurement of price sensitivity, enabling detection of both hedging value (Q premium) and hedging mechanics (equity beta reduction). Hedging could create value via: (1) Financial distress cost reduction (Froot-Scharfstein-Stein) —stable production hedges against bankruptcy; (2) Tax benefits (Jensen's Inequality) ; (3) Agency cost reduction —lower volatility constrains manager risk-shifting. Stock Return Perspective: Hedging mechanically reduces correlation between firm value and commodity prices. Equity beta (systematic risk) should decline . If CAPM holds, lower beta → lower required return → higher valuation. But this reflects risk redistribution to hedging counterparties, not value creation. Data: (1) Hedging activity from 10-K hedge accounting footnotes and management discussion; (2) Oil/gas production from reserve disclosure; (3) Spot prices (WTI crude, Henry Hub natural gas) from EIA; (4) Stock returns from CRSP; (5) Financial data from COMPUSTAT. paper7.title: Why Did the q Theory of Investment Start Working?
paper7.summaryPlain: 1. Core Research Question Andrei, Mann & Moyen address a central puzzle in empirical corporate finance: why has the relationship between aggregate investment and Tobin's q become remarkably tight in recent years after decades of weakness? Historically, q theory failed empirically—investment showed low sensitivity to q and high sensitivity to cash flow. Yet post-2000, the aggregate investment-q relationship strengthened substantially. The authors ask: what economic changes explain this reversal, and are there underlying mechanisms that theory can reveal? 2. Theoretical Framework The authors develop a dynamic investment model augmenting the standard q-theory framework with two distinct mechanisms related to firms' research and innovation activities: (1) innovation jumps in cash flows—firms experience discrete, lumpy improvements in profitability from successful innovations, and (2) learning or frequent updating of beliefs about future cash flows. These mechanisms create endogenous dispersion in Tobin's q across firms. The theory predicts that the investment-q relationship will be stronger in periods with greater empirical dispersion in q, because dispersion indicates sharper differentiation of firms\' investment opportunities. The key insight is that as the economy shifts toward more innovation-intensive activities (particularly in high-tech sectors where q dispersion is naturally high), the aggregate investment-q relationship should tighten. The model shows that with innovation-driven uncertainty and learning, firm-level q becomes more informative about true investment opportunities, strengthening the relationship between investment and q at both firm and aggregate levels. 3. Data & Methodology The study uses comprehensive data from 1955 to 2015 on publicly listed U.S. firms, tracking both the cross-sectional and time-series evolution of Tobin's q. paper8.title: Product Life Cycles in Corporate Finance
paper8.summaryPlain: 1. Core Research Question Hoberg & Maksimovic ask a fundamental question about investment behavior: does a firm's investment sensitivity to Tobin's q depend on which stage of the product life cycle the firm is in? Traditional investment-q regressions treat all firms identically, yet firms at different product life cycle stages face very different investment opportunities and constraints. A startup building new R&D-intensive products (early stage) has fundamentally different investment motives than a mature firm maintaining market share (middle stage) or a declining firm managing divestiture (late stage). The authors develop a novel 10-K text-based measure of product life cycles and test whether conditioning on life cycle substantially improves q theory's explanatory power. They further ask: does life cycle predict the composition of investment types (R&D vs. CAPX vs. acquisitions vs. divestitures)? 2. Theoretical Framework The authors build on Abernathy & Utterback's (1978) classic framework of product life cycles, which posits four distinct stages: (1) Life1 - Fluid/Emergent phase with high innovation, uncertain designs, R&D-heavy investment, (2) Life2 - Transitional phase as dominant designs emerge, both R&D and CAPX important, (3) Life3 - Mature phase with stable products, focus on manufacturing efficiency and CAPX, and (4) Life4 - Decline phase with obsolescence, divestiture, and product extension. The theory predicts that investment sensitivity to q (the regression coefficient β in investment = α + β·q) will vary dramatically across stages: highest in Life1-Life2 (where market valuations reflect growth prospects) and potentially inverted in Life4 (where declining firms may divest high-q assets). The framework also predicts the composition of investments: Life1 firms show high R&D sensitivity to q, Life2 firms show both R&D and CAPX sensitivity, Life3 firms show primarily CAPX sensitivity, and Life4 firms show high divestiture sensitivity. paper9.title: Do Going-Private Transactions Affect Plant Efficiency and Investment?
paper9.summaryPlain: 1. Core Research Question Bharath, Dittmar & Sivadasan examine a fundamental question about the costs and benefits of public versus private ownership: do going-private transactions improve plant-level operational efficiency? There is a longstanding debate: do public markets impose constraints that force firms to operate inefficiently (the "myopia" hypothesis), or are public markets efficient monitors such that going private provides limited efficiency gains? The authors test this using a unique identification strategy: they examine plant-level productivity changes for firms that go private, comparing to control groups of plants in similar industries and sizes. This plant-level approach is novel because previous literature focused on aggregate firm-level metrics. The research connects to broader questions: do public market pressures constrain long-term investments or efficient restructuring? 2. Theoretical Framework The paper draws on competing theoretical predictions about public versus private ownership. The "agency cost" theory predicts that public ownership imposes agency costs (dispersed shareholders, short-term incentives, monitoring costs) that lead to operational inefficiency. Going private would reduce agency costs through: (1) concentrated ownership with aligned incentives, (2) reduced pressure for short-term earnings, and (3) ability to restructure operations without quarterly earnings concerns. By this view, going-private firms should exhibit improved plant productivity relative to remaining public firms. The "efficient market" view argues that public markets efficiently price assets and monitor management, so going private provides minimal efficiency benefits beyond financial engineering. The authors also consider the "selection effect": maybe only efficient firms go private (selection bias), so apparent efficiency gains reflect firm quality rather than the effect of going private. paper10.title: The Operational Consequences of Private Equity Buyouts: Evidence from the Restaurant Industry
paper10.summaryPlain: 1. Core Research Question Bernstein & Sheen ask a critical question about private equity's operational impact: do PE firms actually improve the operations of their portfolio companies, or do they merely apply financial engineering and cost-cutting? The paper investigates this using a novel empirical approach centered on the restaurant industry, leveraging health inspection records as a detailed operational metric. Specifically: do restaurant operational practices (food safety, cleanliness, maintenance) improve after PE buyout, and if so, is this improvement driven by direct PE involvement (operational engineering) or merely selection of better-managed restaurants? The broader question: does PE create value through active operational improvement or primarily through financial restructuring and selection? 2. Theoretical Framework The authors develop a simple framework distinguishing three mechanisms by which PE might affect operations: (1) Selection Effect—PE targets already-efficient operators, so apparent improvements reflect selection rather than PE-induced changes; (2) Operational Engineering—PE actively engages management in improving operations, installing monitoring systems, adopting best practices; (3) Financial Constraints—post-buyout leverage constrains operational investments, reducing operations improvements. The theory predicts different empirical signatures for each mechanism. Selection would show pre-existing trends favoring PE targets before transaction. Operational engineering predicts improvements specific to areas under PE control and stronger when PE partners have relevant industry expertise. Financial constraints predict post-transaction declines in capital/labor intensity despite improved profitability.
